1.pericope_label: The Blessedness of the One Who Fears YHWH
1.synthesis: Psalm 112 is the companion acrostic to Psalm 111, applying to the righteous person the very character qualities that Psalm 111 ascribed to YHWH himself. The beatitude of verse 1 opens the psalm: “Blessed is the man who fears the Lord, who greatly delights in his commandments!” What follows is a portrait of the God-fearer’s life: his descendants will be mighty, wealth and riches are in his house, and “his righteousness endures forever” (v. 3)—the exact phrase used of YHWH in Psalm 111:3. The righteous person is characterized by generosity: he lends freely, conducts his affairs with justice (v. 5), and gives to the poor (v. 9). The psalm’s central promise is psychological: “He is not afraid of bad news; his heart is firm, trusting in the Lord” (v. 7). The closing verse provides a foil: “The wicked man sees it and is angry; he gnashes his teeth and melts away” (v. 10), because the desire of the wicked comes to nothing.
1.historical_context: The verbal mirroring between Psalm 111 and Psalm 112 is theologically programmatic: the same “righteousness endures forever” (111:3 = 112:3), the same “his work is done in faithfulness and justice” (111:7) reflected in the righteous person who “conducts his affairs with justice” (112:5), and the same “established forever” (111:8 = 112:8). The design is unmistakable: the God-fearer becomes like the God he fears, reflecting the divine character into human community. Paul quotes Psalm 112:9 in 2 Corinthians 9:9 to ground the theology of Christian generosity: “He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever.”
1.literary_structure: Psalm 112 mirrors the structure of Psalm 111 but applies each quality to the human God-fearer. The beatitude opening (v. 1) is followed by the outcomes of God-fearing (vv. 2–3), then the specific practices of generosity and justice (vv. 4–6), then the psychological stability of the righteous (vv. 7–8), then the concrete practice of giving to the poor (v. 9), and finally the contrasting fate of the wicked (v. 10). The foil of verse 10—the wicked person seeing the righteous flourish and gnashing his teeth—is the negative confirmation of the beatitude: the wicked’s desire comes to nothing precisely because it is oriented toward what the righteous person has rather than toward YHWH himself.
1.theological_application: Psalm 112’s central psychological promise—“He is not afraid of bad news; his heart is firm, trusting in the Lord” (v. 7)—is grounded in ontology, not technique. The God-fearer’s stability is not a cultivated emotional state but the fruit of a rightly ordered relationship with YHWH. Paul’s quotation of verse 9 in 2 Corinthians 9 connects this to generosity: the person whose heart is fixed on YHWH rather than on personal security gives freely, and this generosity is itself the fruit of the God-fearer’s fundamental trust that YHWH is their provision, not their accumulated wealth.
1.christology: Psalm 112 describes the ideal God-fearer, which Jesus exemplified perfectly. He did not fear bad news; he set his face toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51) with a heart fixed on the Father’s purpose. His “righteousness endures forever” is the ground of the believer’s justification (the imputed righteousness of Christ). His generosity to the poor was comprehensive: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor” (2 Cor 8:9). The light that shines in darkness for the upright (v. 4) is Christ himself, the light that the darkness has not overcome (John 1:5), and those who are united to him begin to carry that light into the darkness around them.
